59	\section{Setup and statement}
60	\label{sec:albaneseup_setup}
61	
62	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
63	irreducible curve \(C\) over \(\bar k\) of genus \(g = g(C) > 0\) (the positive-genus
64	hypothesis is essential: it is precisely the standing hypothesis of Milne's
65	\S III.6). Fix moreover a \(\bar k\)-rational point \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) to serve
66	as the basepoint of the Albanese construction.
67	
68	By the A.3 row of Route~A (the identity-component construction applied to the
69	representable Picard scheme of
70	\cref{def:pic_scheme} in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) the
71	identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \subseteq \Pic_{C/k}\) is a smooth proper
72	geometrically irreducible \(\bar k\)-group scheme of dimension \(g\); we write
73	\(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) and let \(\eta_J \colon \Spec \bar k \to J\) denote its
74	identity section, in the project's convention the chosen \(\mathbf 1\)-pointed
75	morphism that represents the unit element of the underlying group object on
76	\(J\).
77	
78	The universal \(T\)-points of \(J\) are degree-\(0\) line bundles on $C \times_{\bar k}
79	T\(, taken modulo line bundles pulled back from \)T$ (\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback};
80	the identity component then refines this to the degree-zero subfunctor on the
81	\'etale sheafification). This moduli interpretation is what underlies the
82	Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) of
83	\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The universal homomorphism $\psi \colon J
84	\to A\( corresponding to a pointed morphism \)\varphi \colon C \to A$ is
85	constructed by Milne's symmetric-power route: the symmetric assignment
86	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) defines a
87	regular morphism \(\widetilde \psi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\), the
88	birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\) inverts to a
89	rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), and the rational-map extension theorem
90	\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} promotes that rational map to the
91	desired regular morphism \(\psi\).
92	
93	The goal of the chapter is to establish:
94	
95	\begin{theorem}
96	
97	\leanok
98	[Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) -- Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1]
99	  \label{thm:albanese_universal_property}
100	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese_universal_property}
101	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, lem:symmetric_product_av_map,
102	        lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian, lem:descent_through_birational_sigma,
103	        thm:rational_map_to_av_extends, thm:rigidity_lemma}
104	
105	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, doc p.~104
106	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
108	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
109	  %  \(f^P\colon C\,\to\,J\) has the following universal property: for any
110	  %  map \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending
111	  %  \(P\) to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such
112	  %  that \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
113	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1, \S III.6, p.~104.}
114	
115	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over an algebraically
116	  closed field \(\bar k\), let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a \(\bar k\)-rational point,
117	  and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\), \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the
118	  Abel--Jacobi morphism of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. Then for every
119	  abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) and every pointed morphism
120	  \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) with \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\), there exists a unique
121	  homomorphism of group schemes \(\psi \colon J \to A\) such that
122	  \(\varphi = \psi \circ \iota_{P_0}\).
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	The rest of this chapter develops the sub-lemmas
126	(\cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}, \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
127	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}, \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian},
128	and \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}) that the proof rests on,
129	then gives the proof.
130	
131	\section{The Abel--Jacobi morphism at a marked point}
132	\label{sec:albaneseup_abel_jacobi}
133	
134	The first sub-lemma packages the construction and the elementary regularity of
135	the Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\) at the basepoint \(P_0\). It is the
136	content gated on A.3 (existence of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a representable group
137	scheme).
138	
139	\begin{lemma}\leanok
140	[Abel--Jacobi morphism at a basepoint]
141	  \label{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}
142	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}
143	  \uses{def:pic_scheme}
144	
145	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, the canonical map
146	  % \(f^P\colon C\to J\) (introduction to Proposition~6.1), doc p.~104
147	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
148	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
149	  % "PROPOSITION 6.1. Let \(P\) be a \(k\)-rational point on \(C\).  The map
150	  %  \(f^P\colon C\to J\) has the following universal property: for any map
151	  %  \(\varphi\colon C\to A\) from \(C\) into an abelian variety sending \(P\)
152	  %  to \(0\), there is a unique homomorphism \(\psi\colon J\to A\) such that
153	  %  \(\varphi=\psi\circ f^P\)."
154	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S III.6, Summary 6.11, doc p.~107
155	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 113).
156	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
157	  % "SUMMARY 6.11.  Between \((C,P)\) and itself, there is a divisorial
158	  %  correspondence \(\mathcal L^P = \mathcal L(\Delta-\{P\}\times C-C\times\{P\})\).
159	  %    Between \((C,P)\) and \(J\) there is the divisorial correspondence
160	  %  \(\mathcal M^P\); for any divisorial correspondence \(\mathcal L\) between
161	  %  \((C,P)\) and a pointed \(k\)-scheme \((T,t)\), there is a unique morphism
162	  %  of pointed \(k\)-schemes \(\varphi\colon T\to J\) such that
163	  %  \((1\times \varphi)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L\).  In particular,
164	  %  there is a unique map \(f^P\colon C\to J\) such that
165	  %  \((1\times f^P)^*\mathcal M^P\approx \mathcal L^P\) and \(f(P)=0\)."
166	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~6.1 and
167	  Summary~6.11, \S III.6.}
168	
169	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\), let
170	  \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\). There is a
171	  unique regular morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
172	  \[
173	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J, \qquad
174	    Q \;\longmapsto\; \bigl[\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\bigr],
175	  \]
176	  sending the basepoint \(P_0\) to the identity element \(\eta_J \in J(\bar k)\)
177	  and characterised on \(T\)-points by the formula
178	  \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\), where
179	  $\mathcal L^{P_0} := \mathcal O_{C \times C}\bigl(\Delta - \{P_0\} \times C -
180	  C \times \{P_0\}\bigr)$ is the rigidified diagonal correspondence on
181	  \(C \times C\) and \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) is the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence
182	  on \(C \times J\).
183	\end{lemma}
184	
185	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (no separate verbatim source; the existence of
186	% \(f^P\) is part of Milne's standing setup at the head of \S III.6 -- ``\(J\)
187	% will be its Jacobian variety'' -- and is the moduli-of-degree-0-line-bundles
188	% interpretation of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) packaged into Summary~6.11.)
189	\begin{proof}
190	  
191	  By A.3, \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) is the moduli scheme of degree-zero line bundles
192	  on \(C\); concretely, for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) its \(T\)-points are
193	  isomorphism classes of line bundles \(\mathcal L\) on \(C \times_{\bar k} T\)
194	  whose fibre degree at every geometric point of \(T\) is zero, taken modulo line
195	  bundles pulled back from \(T\) (the universal property of the Picard scheme
196	  packaged in \cref{def:pic_scheme}; the identity component imposes the
197	  degree-zero condition).
198	
199	  Apply this to \(T := C\) and the line bundle
200	  \[
201	    \mathcal L^{P_0} \;:=\; \mathcal O_{C \times_{\bar k} C}\bigl(\Delta -
202	      \{P_0\} \times C - C \times \{P_0\}\bigr),
203	  \]
204	  the rigidified diagonal correspondence on \(C \times_{\bar k} C\). Its fibre
205	  over a \(\bar k\)-point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) along the second projection is the
206	  degree-zero line bundle \(\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)\) on \(C\), so \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\)
207	  is a relative degree-zero line bundle, hence a \(C\)-point of \(J\). The
208	  representability of \(J\) at \(T = C\) produces the unique morphism
209	  \[
210	    \iota_{P_0} \;\colon\; C \;\longrightarrow\; J
211	  \]
212	  such that \((1 \times \iota_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
213	  for the Poincar\'e--Picard correspondence \(\mathcal M^{P_0}\) on \(C \times J\).
214	
215	  At the basepoint, restriction of \(\mathcal L^{P_0}\) to \(C \times \{P_0\}\)
216	  gives \(\mathcal O_C(P_0 - P_0) \cong \mathcal O_C\), the trivial line bundle,
217	  whose moduli class in \(J(\bar k)\) is the identity element \(\eta_J\). Hence
218	  \(\iota_{P_0}(P_0) = \eta_J\). Uniqueness of \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the moduli
219	  uniqueness clause of representability: any other morphism \(\iota'_{P_0}\)
220	  satisfying \((1 \times \iota'_{P_0})^*\mathcal M^{P_0} \approx \mathcal L^{P_0}\)
221	  and the pointed condition agrees with \(\iota_{P_0}\) on \(T\)-points for every
222	  \(T\), hence equals \(\iota_{P_0}\) by the Yoneda embedding.
223	\end{proof}
224	
225	\section{The \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)}
226	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g}
227	
228	The Albanese universal property is built from \(C\) by taking the
229	\(g\)-th \emph{symmetric power} \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) -- the quotient of the
230	\(g\)-fold Cartesian power \(C^g\) by the natural action of the symmetric group
231	\(S_g\). We collect the definition here as the first sub-lemma block.
232	
233	\begin{definition}\leanok
234	[Symmetric power of a curve]
235	  \label{def:symmetric_power_curve}
236	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}
237	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition~3.1, \S III.3,
238	  % doc p.~94 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 100).
239	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
240	  % "For any variety \(V\), the symmetric group \(S_r\) on \(r\) letters acts on
241	  %  the product of \(r\) copies \(V^r\) of \(V\) by permuting the factors, and
242	  %  we want to define the \(r\)th symmetric power \(V^{(r)}\) of \(V\) to be the
243	  %  quotient \(S_r \backslash V^r\).  The next proposition demonstrates the
244	  %  existence of \(V^{(r)}\) and lists its main properties.
245	  %    A morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) is said to be {\bf symmetric} if
246	  %  \(\varphi\sigma=\varphi\) for all \(\sigma\) in \(S_r\).
247	  %  PROPOSITION 3.1. Let \(V\) be a variety over \(k\).  Then there exists a
248	  %  variety \(V^{(r)}\) and a symmetric morphism \(\pi\colon V^r\to V^{(r)}\)
249	  %  having the following properties:
250	  %    (a) as a topological space, \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) is the quotient of \(V^r\)
251	  %        by \(S_r\);
252	  %    (b) for any open affine subset \(U\) of \(V\), \(U^{(r)}\) is an open
253	  %        affine subset of \(V^{(r)}\) and
254	  %             $\Gamma(U^{(r)},\mathcal O_{V^{(r)}}) = \Gamma(U^r,
255	  %             \mathcal O_{V^r})^{S_r}$
256	  %        (set of elements fixed by the action of \(S_r\)).
257	  %  The pair \((V^{(r)},\pi)\) has the following universal property: every
258	  %  symmetric \(k\)-morphism \(\varphi\colon V^r\to T\) factors uniquely
259	  %  through \(\pi\).
260	  %  The map \(\pi\) is finite, surjective, and separable."
261	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proposition~3.1, \S III.3, p.~94.}
262	
263	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve over an algebraically closed field
264	  \(\bar k\) and let \(g \ge 1\). The \emph{\(g\)-th symmetric power}
265	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is a \(\bar k\)-scheme equipped with a finite surjective
266	  symmetric morphism \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) characterised by
267	  the following universal property: for every \(\bar k\)-scheme \(T\) and every
268	  symmetric \(\bar k\)-morphism \(\varphi \colon C^g \to T\) (i.e.\ a morphism
269	  satisfying \(\varphi \circ \sigma = \varphi\) for all \(\sigma \in S_g\)
270	  acting by permutation of the factors of \(C^g\)) there exists a unique
271	  morphism \(\overline\varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to T\) with
272	  \(\overline\varphi \circ \pi = \varphi\). As a topological space
273	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) is the quotient of \(C^g\) by \(S_g\), and for every open
274	  affine \(U \subseteq C\) the open subset $U^{(g)} \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g
275	  C\( is affine with global sections the \)S_g$-invariants
276	  \(\Gamma(U^g, \mathcal O_{C^g})^{S_g}\).
277	\end{definition}
278	
279	\medskip
280	
281	\textbf{Mathlib status.} Mathlib has no formalised \(g\)-th symmetric power of
282	a scheme: only \texttt{Sym}/\texttt{SymmetricPower} for types and modules.
283	The corresponding scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied
284	project-side. The intended Lean construction follows Milne's recipe of
285	\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}: on affine open \(U = \Spec A \subseteq C\)
286	form \(\Spec(A^{\otimes g})^{S_g}\), then glue via the standard affine
287	patching for an open cover \(C = \bigcup U_i\). This sub-build is a
288	non-trivial dependency of A.4.d that the prover lane must absorb before the
289	main universal property closes; a \texttt{\textbackslash notready} marker is
290	appropriate on the corresponding Lean target until the construction lands.
291	For the proof of Proposition~3.1 itself, Milne refers to Mumford 1970,
292	\S II~\S 7, p.~66 and \S III \S 11, p.~112, for the patching details (the
293	quoted reference at the end of Milne's Proof on \S III.3, p.~94).
294	
295	\section{The morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)}
296	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_av_map}
297	
298	The second sub-lemma is the elementary fact that the symmetric assignment
299	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\) from
300	\(C^g\) to \(A\) descends through \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\). The
301	key point is that the addition morphism \(A^g \to A\) is itself
302	\(S_g\)-symmetric because \(A\) is commutative as a group scheme.
303	
304	\begin{lemma}\leanok
305	[Symmetrisation of a curve-to-AV morphism]
306	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_av_map}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}
308	  \uses{def:symmetric_power_curve}
309	
310	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
311	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
312	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
313	  % "PROOF.  Consider the map
314	  %     \((P_1,...,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\)
315	  %  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\)."
316	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
317	  \S III.6, p.~104.}
318	
319	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
320	  over \(\bar k\). The composition
321	  \[
322	    \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}
323	    \;\colon\; C^g \;\longrightarrow\; A^g \;\longrightarrow\; A,
324	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\; \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g),
325	  \]
326	  is \(S_g\)-equivariant (where \(S_g\) acts on \(C^g\) by permutation of the
327	  factors and on \(A\) trivially). Consequently, by the universal property of
328	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) (\cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}), there is a unique
329	  morphism of \(\bar k\)-schemes
330	  \[
331	    \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A
332	  \]
333	  such that
334	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ \pi = \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\).
335	\end{lemma}
336	
337	\begin{proof}
338	  
339	  Since \(A\) is a commutative group scheme, the \(g\)-fold addition morphism
340	  \(\mathrm{add}_A \colon A^g \to A\) satisfies $\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau =
341	  \mathrm{add}_A\( for every \)\tau \in S_g\( permuting the factors of \)A^g$
342	  (this is the scheme-theoretic statement that summation in a commutative
343	  monoid is independent of the order of summands; on \(T\)-points it is the
344	  identity \(a_1 + \cdots + a_g = a_{\tau(1)} + \cdots + a_{\tau(g)}\) in the
345	  abelian group \(A(T)\)). The diagonal action of \(\tau\) on $\varphi^{\times
346	  g} \colon C^g \to A^g$ is also given by permutation of factors. Therefore
347	  \[
348	    (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}) \circ \tau
349	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\varphi^{\times g} \circ \tau)
350	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ (\tau \circ \varphi^{\times g})
351	    \;=\; (\mathrm{add}_A \circ \tau) \circ \varphi^{\times g}
352	    \;=\; \mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g},
353	  \]
354	  so the composition \(\mathrm{add}_A \circ \varphi^{\times g}\) is symmetric
355	  in the sense of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}. The universal property
356	  of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the unique factorisation
357	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\).
358	\end{proof}
359	
360	\section{The birational morphism \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \dashrightarrow J\)}
361	\label{sec:albaneseup_sym_g_to_jacobian}
362	
363	The third sub-lemma is the birational identification of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)
364	with \(J\). After choosing the basepoint \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\), the cycle map
365	\((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\)
366	defines a morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) which is
367	birational by Milne III~Theorem~5.1(a).
368	
369	\begin{lemma}\leanok
370	[Birational morphism from \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to \(J\)]
371	  \label{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}
372	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}
373	  \uses{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism, def:symmetric_power_curve}
374	
375	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~5.1(a), \S III.5,
376	  % doc p.~101 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 107).
377	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
378	  % "Let \(f^r\) be the map \(C^r\to J\) sending \((P_1,...,P_r)\) to
379	  %  \(f(P_1)+\cdots+f(P_r)\).  On points, \(f^r\) is the map
380	  %  \((P_1,...,P_r)\mapsto[P_1+\cdots+P_r-rP]\).  Clearly it is symmetric,
381	  %  and so induces a map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to J\).  We can regard
382	  %  \(f^{(r)}\) as being the map sending an effective divisor \(D\) of degree
383	  %  \(r\) on \(C\) to the linear equivalence class of \(D-rP\).  The fibre of
384	  %  the map \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}(k)\to J(k)\) containing \(D\) can be
385	  %  identified with the space of effective divisors linearly equivalent
386	  %  to \(D\), that is, with the linear system \(|D|\).  The image of \(C^{(r)}\)
387	  %  in \(J\) is a closed subvariety \(W^r\) of \(J\), which can also be written
388	  %  \(W^r=f(C)+\cdots+f(C)\) (\(r\) summands).
389	  %  THEOREM 5.1.  (a) For all \(r\le g\), the morphism
390	  %  \(f^{(r)}\colon C^{(r)}\to W^r\) is birational; in particular,
391	  %  \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational map from \(C^{(g)}\) onto \(J\)."
392	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~5.1(a) and the
393	  preceding paragraph, \S III.5, p.~101.}
394	
395	  Let \(C\) be a complete nonsingular curve of genus \(g > 0\) over \(\bar k\),
396	  let \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\) be a basepoint, and write \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\),
397	  \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to J\) for the Abel--Jacobi morphism of
398	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}. The regular morphism
399	  \(\iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J^g\) composed with the \(g\)-fold
400	  group-law addition \(\mathrm{add}_J \colon J^g \to J\) descends through
401	  \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) to a regular morphism
402	  \[
403	    f^{(g)} \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; J,
404	    \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_g) \;\longmapsto\;
405	      \bigl[\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)\bigr].
406	  \]
407	  The morphism \(f^{(g)}\) is birational: it is dominant (its image is
408	  \(J\), the \(g\)-fold sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) exhausts
409	  \(J\) since \(\dim J = g\) and the iterated sum reaches full dimension), and
410	  its generic fibre is a single \(\bar k\)-point (a degree-\(g\) effective
411	  divisor \(D\) on \(C\) with \(h^0(D) = 1\) is the unique element of its linear
412	  system \(|D|\); such divisors form a dense open subset of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
413	  C\( -- a consequence of \)\dim |D| = 0\( generically on a curve of genus \)g$
414	  by Riemann--Roch). Equivalently, \(f^{(g)}\) is a birational morphism in
415	  the sense that there exist dense opens
416	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\), \(V \subseteq J\) such that
417	  \(f^{(g)}|_U \colon U \to V\) is an isomorphism.
418	\end{lemma}
419	
420	% NOTE (iter-199): the birationality proof below derives the generic-fibre
421	% identification from Riemann--Roch at \(\deg D = g\) (specifically
422	% \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = 1\) generically). Riemann--Roch is the Route C
423	% chain (\texttt{RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex} et al.), currently PAUSED
424	% per USER 2026-05-28 standing directive. This lemma is therefore
425	% gated on Route C re-engagement (or on a parallel construction that
426	% sidesteps RR). Flagged here so a future prover does not attempt to
427	% formalize the birationality argument until the RR substrate is
428	% available.
429	
430	\begin{proof}
431	
432	  Symmetry of \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g}\) is the
433	  specialisation of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} to the abelian
434	  variety \(A := J\) and the morphism \(\varphi := \iota_{P_0}\); the universal
435	  property of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) produces the regular morphism \(f^{(g)}\).
436	  The closed-form description on \(\bar k\)-points,
437	  \(f^{(g)}(P_1, \ldots, P_g) = [\mathcal O_C(P_1 + \cdots + P_g - g P_0)]\),
438	  follows because \(\iota_{P_0}(P_i) = [\mathcal O_C(P_i - P_0)]\) by
439	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} and the group law on \(J\) is the
440	  tensor-product of degree-zero line bundles.
441	
442	  We verify birationality. The image of \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is the iterated
443	  sum \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) summands), which is
444	  closed (it is the image of the proper morphism
445	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) being proper because \(C^g\)
446	  is proper and \(\pi\) is finite surjective). By the dimension count
447	  \(\dim \mathrm{Sym}^g C = g = \dim J\) (Riemann--Roch and the standing
448	  hypothesis \(g > 0\)), the image \(W^g \subseteq J\) has dimension at most
449	  \(g\), and equality forces \(W^g = J\) since \(J\) is irreducible. Hence
450	  \(f^{(g)}\) is surjective with \(\dim\)-equal source and target.
451	
452	  For the generic-fibre identification, Riemann--Roch
453	  \(h^0(D) - h^1(D) = \deg(D) + 1 - g\) at \(\deg D = g\) gives $h^0(D) - h^1(D)
454	  = 1\(, so for a general \)D \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\( with \)h^1(D) =
455	  0\( (the locus where \)h^1$ jumps is closed of positive codimension on a
456	  smooth curve, by upper semicontinuity of cohomology), we have $h^0(D) =
457	  1\( and the linear system \)|D|\( consists of \)D$ alone. Therefore the
458	  fibre of \(f^{(g)}\) over the generic point of \(J\) contains a single point
459	  \(D\), i.e.\ \(f^{(g)}\) is birational onto \(J\).
460	
461	  By \cref{def:pic_scheme} the underlying \(\bar k\)-scheme \(J\) is the
462	  Picard scheme \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) obtained at A.3, so the moduli-theoretic
463	  reading of \(f^{(g)}\) (effective divisor class \(\mapsto\) degree-zero
464	  line bundle class via \(D \mapsto [\mathcal O_C(D - g P_0)]\)) is the
465	  same as the Yoneda image of the \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\)-point
466	  $[\mathcal O_{C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g C}(\mathcal D_g - g\{P_0\} \times
467	  \mathrm{Sym}^g C)]\(, where \)\mathcal D_g \subseteq C \times \mathrm{Sym}^g
468	  C\( is the universal effective Cartier divisor of degree \)g\( on \)C$. We
469	  do not need this Yoneda repackaging for the proof of the Albanese UP --
470	  the symmetric-power route uses only the morphism \(f^{(g)}\) and its
471	  birationality.
472	\end{proof}
473	
474	\section{Descent through the birational quotient \(\sigma\)}
475	\label{sec:albaneseup_descent}
476	
477	The final auxiliary lemma packages the two-step descent that promotes a
478	morphism on \(C^g\) (after passing through \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) via
479	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and inverting \(f^{(g)}\) via
480	\cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}) to a regular morphism on
481	\(J\). The output is a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\), which is then
482	upgraded to a regular morphism via Milne's Theorem~3.2
483	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}). This is the unique step of the
484	proof that consumes A.4.c.
485	
486	\begin{lemma}\leanok
487	[Descent of \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\)]
488	  \label{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma}
489	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}
490	  \uses{lem:symmetric_product_av_map, lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian,
491	        thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
492	
493	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proof of Proposition~6.1, \S III.6,
494	  % doc p.~104 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 110).
495	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
496	  % "It therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
497	  %  shows to be a regular map."
498	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Proof of Proposition~6.1,
499	  \S III.6, p.~104 (the sentence ``which (I~3.2) shows to be a regular
500	  map'').}
501	
502	  Let \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) be a morphism into an abelian variety \(A\)
503	  over \(\bar k\). Let \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)
504	  be the symmetrised morphism of \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} and
505	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) the birational morphism of
506	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}. Then there is a unique
507	  regular morphism
508	  \[
509	    \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A
510	  \]
511	  such that \(\psi \circ f^{(g)} = \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi\) on the dense open
512	  on which \(f^{(g)}\) is an isomorphism (equivalently, \(\psi\) is the unique
513	  regular morphism extending the rational map
514	  $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1} \colon J \dashrightarrow
515	  A$).
516	\end{lemma}
517	
518	\begin{proof}
519	  
520	  By \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, there exist dense opens
521	  \(U \subseteq \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and \(V \subseteq J\) with $f^{(g)}|_U \colon
522	  U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\( an isomorphism. Setting \)\psi_0 \colon V \to A$
523	  to be the composition $\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi|_U \circ (f^{(g)}|_U)^{-1}
524	  \colon V \to U \to A\( produces a rational map \)J \dashrightarrow A$.
525	
526	  The target \(A\) is an abelian variety and the source \(J\) is smooth over
527	  \(\bar k\) (it is itself an abelian variety, hence nonsingular). Milne's
528	  Theorem~3.2 (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) therefore promotes
529	  \(\psi_0\) to a unique regular morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\) extending
530	  \(\psi_0\) on \(V\). Uniqueness of \(\psi\) follows from the
531	  reduced-and-separated agreement principle: two regular morphisms
532	  \(J \to A\) agreeing on a dense open are equal.
533	\end{proof}
534	
535	\section{Proof of the universal property}
536	\label{sec:albaneseup_proof}
537	
538	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
539	% "PROOF.  Consider the map
540	%    \(\)(P_1,\ldots,P_g)\mapsto\sum_i\psi(P_i)\colon C^g\to A.\(\)
541	%  Clearly this is symmetric, and so it factors through \(C^{(g)}\).  It
542	%  therefore defines a rational map \(\psi\colon J\to A\), which (I 3.2)
543	%  shows to be a regular map.  It is clear from the construction that
544	%  \(\psi\circ f^P=\varphi\) (note that \(f^P\) is the composite of $Q\mapsto
545	%  Q+(g-1)P\colon C\to C^{(g)}\( with \)f^{(g)}\colon C^{(g)}\to J$).  In
546	%  particular, \(\psi\) maps 0 to 0, and (I 1.2) shows that it is therefore
547	%  a homomorphism.  If \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism such that
548	%  \(\psi'\circ f^P=\varphi\), then \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on
549	%  \(f^P(C)+\cdots+f^P(C)\) (\(g\) copies), which is the whole of \(J\)."
550	\begin{proof}[Proof of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}]
551	  
552	  The proof follows Milne's Proposition~6.1 verbatim, using the four
553	  sub-lemmas of this chapter:
554	  \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism} (Abel--Jacobi morphism \(\iota_{P_0}\)),
555	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} (symmetrisation of \(\varphi\) to
556	  \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\)),
557	  \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian} (birational morphism
558	  \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\)), and
559	  \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} (descent of $\mathrm{Sym}^g
560	  \varphi\( to a regular morphism \)\psi \colon J \to A$ via
561	  \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}).
562	  \begin{enumerate}
563	    \item \emph{Symmetrisation.} Consider the map \(C^g \to A\) sending
564	      \((P_1, \ldots, P_g) \mapsto \varphi(P_1) + \cdots + \varphi(P_g)\)
565	      (Milne's typeset proof writes \(\sum_i \psi(P_i)\) in this line, but
566	      the construction is the symmetrisation of the input \(\varphi\); we
567	      use the unambiguous symbol \(\varphi\) throughout). Since \(A\) is
568	      commutative this map is \(S_g\)-symmetric. By
569	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} it factors through the
570	      symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\), defining a regular morphism
571	      \[
572	        \widetilde \psi \;:=\; \mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi
573	        \;\colon\; \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\longrightarrow\; A.
574	      \]
575	    \item \emph{Descent to a regular morphism on \(J\).} By
576	      \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, the morphism
577	      \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) is birational. The
578	      composite \(\widetilde \psi \circ (f^{(g)})^{-1}\) is, a priori,
579	      only a rational map \(J \dashrightarrow A\). By
580	      \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma} -- whose proof is an
581	      application of \cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends} (Milne
582	      Theorem~I~3.2, rational map from a nonsingular variety to an
583	      abelian variety is regular) -- this rational map extends uniquely
584	      to a regular morphism
585	      \[
586	        \psi \;\colon\; J \;\longrightarrow\; A.
587	      \]
588	    \item \emph{Factorisation \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\).}
589	      Milne's identification: \(\iota_{P_0}\) is the composite
590	      \[
591	        C \;\xrightarrow{\;Q \mapsto Q + (g-1) P_0\;}\;
592	        \mathrm{Sym}^g C \;\xrightarrow{\;f^{(g)}\;}\; J,
593	      \]
594	      where the first arrow sends a point \(Q \in C(\bar k)\) to the
595	      effective divisor \(Q + (g-1) P_0 \in \mathrm{Sym}^g C(\bar k)\)
596	      (its image under \(f^{(g)}\) in \(J\) is
597	      $[\mathcal O_C(Q + (g-1) P_0 - g P_0)] = [\mathcal O_C(Q - P_0)]
598	      = \iota_{P_0}(Q)$, matching \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}). On
599	      \(\bar k\)-points,
600	      \[
601	        \psi(\iota_{P_0}(Q)) = \widetilde\psi(Q + (g-1) P_0)
602	          = \varphi(Q) + (g-1)\varphi(P_0) = \varphi(Q),
603	      \]
604	      using \(\varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\) and that \(\eta_A\) is the identity of
605	      \(A\). Hence \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) on \(\bar k\)-points,
606	      and therefore \(\psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\) as morphisms of
607	      \(\bar k\)-schemes (both sides are regular morphisms \(C \to A\) from
608	      a reduced separated scheme to a separated scheme, agreeing on
609	      dense \(\bar k\)-points, hence agreeing everywhere by the
610	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle).
611	    \item \emph{Homomorphism property.} The morphism \(\psi\) sends
612	      \(\eta_J\) to \(\eta_A\): indeed,
613	      \(\psi(\eta_J) = \psi(\iota_{P_0}(P_0)) = \varphi(P_0) = \eta_A\).
614	      A regular morphism of abelian varieties sending the identity to
615	      the identity is a homomorphism of group schemes, by Milne
616	      Corollary~I~1.2 (a consequence of the Rigidity Lemma
617	      \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, see
618	      \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}, Corollary~1.2). Therefore
619	      \(\psi\) is a homomorphism of group schemes \(J \to A\).
620	    \item \emph{Uniqueness.} Suppose \(\psi'\) is a second homomorphism
621	      with \(\psi' \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi\). Then \(\psi\) and
622	      \(\psi'\) agree on the set-theoretic image $\iota_{P_0}(C) \subseteq
623	      J$. Both are homomorphisms, so they agree on the subgroup
624	      generated by \(\iota_{P_0}(C)\) -- which contains the \(g\)-fold sum
625	      \(\iota_{P_0}(C) + \cdots + \iota_{P_0}(C)\) (\(g\) copies). The
626	      \(g\)-fold sum is the image of
627	      \(\mathrm{add}_J \circ \iota_{P_0}^{\times g} \colon C^g \to J\),
628	      which descends to \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\), which
629	      is surjective by \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}.
630	      Therefore \(\psi\) and \(\psi'\) agree on a dense open subset of \(J\)
631	      (in fact on all of \(J\) as a set), hence everywhere by the
632	      reduced-and-separated agreement principle.
633	  \end{enumerate}
634	  This completes the proof.
635	\end{proof}
636	
637	\section{Wiring for the positive-genus Albanese witness}
638	\label{sec:albaneseup_witness_wiring}
639	
640	\cref{thm:albanese_universal_property} supplies the universal-factorisation
641	field \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\) of an Albanese witness for \((C, P_0)\) in the
642	sense of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} (\cref{chap:Jacobian}); the remaining
643	fields are supplied by A.3:
644	\begin{itemize}
645	  \item \(J := \Pic^0_{C/k}\) (the underlying scheme).
646	  \item \(\texttt{grpObj}\): the group-object structure on \(J\) obtained from the
647	    abelian-group structure on \(\Pic^0_{C/k}(T)\) for varying \(T\), refined to a
648	    group object in the category of \(\bar k\)-schemes (A.3, A.2.c group-scheme
649	    refinement).
650	  \item \(\texttt{proper}\): properness of \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Spec \bar k\) from
651	    the closed-immersion property of the identity component in the proper
652	    representing Picard scheme (A.3; cf.\
653	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_proper}'s discussion).
654	  \item \(\texttt{smoothGenus}\): smoothness of relative dimension \(g(C)\) from
655	    the deformation-theoretic tangent-space identification with $H^1(C,
656	    \mathcal O_C)\(, which has dimension \)g(C)$ by Riemann--Roch.
657	  \item \(\texttt{geomIrred}\): geometric irreducibility of the identity
658	    component of a \(\bar k\)-group scheme locally of finite type (Kleiman
659	    Lem.~lem:agps and Prp.~prp:pic0; cf.\
660	    \cref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}'s discussion).
661	  \item \(\texttt{isAlbaneseFor}\): the present
662	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}, applied at each
663	    \(P_0 \in C(\bar k)\).
664	\end{itemize}
665	
666	The witness so produced is over \(\bar k\). The Galois-descent step from $\bar
667	k\( back to the original base field \)k$ -- packaged at
668	\cref{chap:Jacobian}, sub-step C.2.f -- closes the loop and supplies the
669	\(k\)-side Albanese witness consumed by \cref{def:Jacobian}.
670	
671	\section{Lean encoding}
672	\label{sec:albaneseup_lean_encoding}
673	
674	The Lean target file is the new
675	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AlbaneseUP.lean} (declared at the top of
676	this chapter). The five target declarations are:
677	\begin{itemize}
678	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}: the regular morphism
679	    \(\iota_{P_0} \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}\) of \cref{lem:abel_jacobi_morphism}.
680	    The signature exposes the marked point \(P_0\) as an explicit argument and
681	    the Picard-scheme construction \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) as an existing object.
682	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.SymmetricPower}: the scheme-theoretic
683	    \(g\)-th symmetric power \(\mathrm{Sym}^g C\) of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve},
684	    packaged with the canonical projection
685	    \(\pi \colon C^g \to \mathrm{Sym}^g C\) and its universal property
686	    (factorisation of every \(S_g\)-symmetric morphism out of \(C^g\)). \emph{This
687	    declaration has no Mathlib analogue}: Mathlib's \texttt{Sym} /
688	    \texttt{SymmetricPower} cover types and modules only; the
689	    scheme-theoretic construction must be supplied project-side, following
690	    Milne's affine-and-glue recipe of \cref{def:symmetric_power_curve}.
691	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerAVMap}: the
692	    symmetrised morphism
693	    \(\mathrm{Sym}^g \varphi \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to A\) produced by
694	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_av_map} from an input morphism
695	    \(\varphi \colon C \to A\) into an abelian variety.
696	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.symmetricPowerToJacobian}: the
697	    birational morphism \(f^{(g)} \colon \mathrm{Sym}^g C \to J\) of
698	    \cref{lem:symmetric_product_to_jacobian}, together with the data of a
699	    dense open isomorphism \(U \xrightarrow{\sim} V\) realising
700	    birationality. The signature exposes the basepoint \(P_0\) and consumes
701	    \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.abelJacobi}.
702	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.descentThroughBirationalSigma}: the
703	    descent lemma of \cref{lem:descent_through_birational_sigma},
704	    consuming \texttt{symmetricPowerAVMap} and
705	    \texttt{symmetricPowerToJacobian} together with
706	    \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
707	    (\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) to produce the regular
708	    morphism \(\psi \colon J \to A\).
709	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Pic0.albanese\_universal\_property}: the
710	    main theorem, the existence-and-uniqueness statement of
711	    \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}. The signature is
712	    \[
713	      \texttt{P\_0 : C(\bar k)} \;\to\;
714	      \texttt{A : AbelianVariety \(\bar k\)} \;\to\;
715	      (\varphi : C \to A) \;\to\;
716	      (h\varphi : \varphi \circ P_0 = \eta_A) \;\to\;
717	      \exists ! \, \psi \colon J \to A, \;\; \psi \circ \iota_{P_0} = \varphi
718	      \;\wedge\; \psi \text{ is a group homomorphism}.
719	    \]
720	\end{itemize}
721	
722	The proof body of \texttt{albanese\_universal\_property} consumes its four
723	sub-lemma exports plus \texttt{Albanese.Thm32RationalMapExtension.extend\_to\_av}
724	(\cref{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}) and Milne's Corollary~I~1.2
725	(\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} consequence, registered at
726	\cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}); the wiring follows the five-step proof
727	of \cref{thm:albanese_universal_property}.
728	
729	The Picard-scheme inputs (\cref{def:pic_scheme} of
730	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}) are imported from

1	\chapter{Auslander--Buchsbaum}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.b sub-row of Route A
6	% (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It packages the
7	% Auslander--Buchsbaum formula and the corollary ``regular local ring
8	% \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay'' that A.4.a (the codim-1 extension proof for
9	% Albanese morphisms out of a regular projective surface) consumes. Per
10	% STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
11	% startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the
12	% Albanese geometry and lives entirely under \texttt{RingTheory.*}. The
13	% target Lean file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean}
14	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
15	% creating it. Note that Mathlib already houses parts of the depth /
16	% projective-dimension API
17	% (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Depth.*},
18	% \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Ideal.RegularSequence},
19	% \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.ProjectiveResolution}); a Plan-Agent audit
20	% at the pinned commit (\texttt{b80f227}) should confirm what is present
21	% before the prover writes the file, since a fully Mathlib-backed
22	% Auslander--Buchsbaum would collapse this chapter to a re-export.
23	
24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (\cref{def:depth} and \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (\cref{def:projective_dimension}),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (\cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (\cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (\cref{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  \uses{def:depth, lem:ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator}
110	
111	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
112	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
113	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
114	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
115	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
116	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
117	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
119	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
120	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
121	  \[
122	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
123	  \]
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
127	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
128	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
129	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
130	%  By Lemma REF we have
131	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
132	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
133	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
134	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
135	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
136	%  Consider the long exact sequence
137	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
138	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
139	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
140	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
141	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
142	%  Lemma REF.
143	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
144	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
145	\begin{proof}
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  
150	  
151	\leanok
152	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
153	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
154	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
155	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
156	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
157	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
158	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
159	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
160	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
161	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
162	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
163	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
164	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
165	\end{proof}
166	
167	\section{Projective dimension}
168	\label{sec:ab_pd}
169	
170	\begin{definition}
171	
172	\leanok
173	  [Projective dimension]
174	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
175	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
176	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
177	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
178	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
179	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
180	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
181	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
182	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
183	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
184	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
185	  \[
186	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
187	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
188	  \]
189	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
190	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
191	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
192	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
193	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} concerns only nonzero \(M\).
194	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
195	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
196	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
197	\end{definition}
198	
199	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
200	\begin{itemize}

1	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean
2	% NOTE: Weil-divisor API extension of RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex --- re-uses
3	% Scheme.WeilDivisor, Scheme.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order from
4	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The order map
5	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order} is not re-defined here;
6	% see \cref{def:order_at_point} for the canonical reference.

8	\chapter{Codimension-1 indeterminacy extension (A.4.a)}
9	\label{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension}
10	
11	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.a sub-row of Route A
12	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). Per
13	% STRATEGY.md L29 it is the \emph{risk-dominant} A.4 sub-row
14	% ("project-fatal if it stalls"). The chapter has two outputs:
15	% (i) \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} --- Milne Theorem~3.1: a rational map
16	%     from a normal variety to a complete variety is defined on the complement
17	%     of a codimension-\(\geq 2\) closed subset. Specialised below to the
18	%     smooth-variety setting where the prover can phrase it as
19	%     "no codim-1 indeterminacy \(\Rightarrow\) extension". This is one of the
20	%     two inputs to Milne Theorem~3.2 in the sibling chapter
21	%     \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}.
22	% (ii) \cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} --- Milne Lemma~3.3: for a
23	%     rational map from a nonsingular variety to a group variety, the
24	%     indeterminacy locus is either empty or of pure codimension \(1\). This is
25	%     the second input to Milne Theorem~3.2; it consumes nothing from A.4.b.
26	% A bonus output is \cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction}: the Weil-divisor
27	% reformulation of (i) that a smooth-surface codim-2 extension is obstructed
28	% exactly by the order of the pulled-back coordinates of \(f\) being negative
29	% at a prime divisor. This is the lemma A.4.a uses to align with the project's
30	% \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor} machinery.
31	%
32	% Depth-\(\geq 2\) extension on a smooth surface, used in the proof body of
33	% \cref{thm:codim_one_extension} when reducing the general-codim case to the
34	% normal-DVR-codim-1 case, consumes
35	% \cref{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay} of
36	% \cref{chap:Albanese_AuslanderBuchsbaum} (the A.4.b sub-row). The chapter
37	% records the dependency but does not re-prove it.
38	%
39	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CodimOneExtension.lean}
40	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
41	% creating it.
42	
43	\section{Setup and motivation}
44	\label{sec:codim1_setup}
45	
46	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\) throughout. By a \emph{variety} over \(\bar k\)
47	we mean a geometrically integral, separated scheme of finite type over \(\bar k\)
48	(Milne's convention, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, p.~v); a variety is \emph{nonsingular}
49	(or \emph{smooth}) if its local ring at every closed point is regular. A variety is
50	\emph{complete} if it is proper over \(\bar k\).
51	
52	A \emph{rational map} \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) between varieties is the equivalence
53	class of a regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to Y\) defined on a dense open subset
54	\(U \subseteq X\), where two pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\), \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) are equivalent
55	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\) (Milne~\S I.3, p.~16). The map \(f\)
56	is \emph{defined at} a point \(x \in X\) if there is some representative \((U, \varphi_U)\)
57	with \(x \in U\); the set of such \(x\) is an open subset \(\mathrm{Dom}(f) \subseteq X\),
58	the \emph{domain of definition} of \(f\).
59	
60	The motivating question of this chapter, following Milne~\S I.3, is:
61	\begin{quote}
62	  \emph{When does a rational map \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow Y\) extend to a regular
63	  morphism on all of \(X\)?}
64	\end{quote}
65	Without hypotheses on \(X\) or \(Y\) the question has no positive answer (Milne lists three
66	counterexamples on p.~16: the inclusion of a proper open into the ambient variety; the
67	rational map to the cuspidal cubic whose inverse fails to extend; the inverse of a
68	blow-up at a smooth point). The two structural inputs that buy unconditional extension
69	in our setting are:
70	\begin{itemize}
71	  \item \emph{Target completeness:} if \(Y\) is complete (proper over \(\bar k\)), the
72	    valuative criterion of properness forces the rational map to extend across every
73	    codim-\(1\) point of a normal source \(X\) (Milne Theorem~3.1).
74	  \item \emph{Target group structure:} if \(Y\) is a group variety, the difference map
75	    \(\Phi(x, y) := f(x) \cdot f(y)^{-1}\) on \(X \times X\) has a vanishing/pole divisor
76	    along the diagonal that controls the codimension of the indeterminacy locus
77	    (Milne Lemma~3.3).
78	\end{itemize}
79	For \(Y\) an abelian variety both conditions hold, and combining them yields Milne's
80	Theorem~3.2 (the headline of \cref{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}). The
81	present chapter packages the two inputs separately:
82	\cref{thm:codim_one_extension} is Milne~3.1, and
83	\cref{lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy} is Milne~3.3. The Weil-divisor reformulation
84	\cref{thm:weil_divisor_obstruction} translates the codim-\(1\) extension obstruction
85	in Milne~3.1's proof into the order-at-a-prime-divisor language pinned in
86	\cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}, which is the form the Lean prover consumes when
87	working with the project's \(\mathrm{Scheme.WeilDivisor}\) API.
88	
89	\section{The indeterminacy locus of a rational map}
90	\label{sec:indeterm_locus}
91	
92	\begin{definition}
93	
94	
95	\leanok
96	  [Indeterminacy locus of a rational map]
97	  \label{def:indeterminacy_locus}
98	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.indeterminacyLocus}
99	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], \S I.3, p.~16, the paragraph "Let \(V\) and \(W\)
100	  % be varieties over \(k\)\ldots" defining the domain of definition of a rational map

3	\chapter{Coheight--Krull dim bridge for scheme points}
4	\label{chap:Albanese_CoheightBridge}
5	
6	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-183). This chapter is a project-side scaffold sitting
7	% between \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} and the Riemann--Roch
8	% Weil-divisor machinery of \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}. The
9	% directive originates from the iter-182 plan-phase api-alignment consult
10	% \texttt{analogies/stacks-00tt-coheight.md} (Decision 2), which determined

12	% prerequisite for the scheme-level coheight \(=\) Krull-dim-of-stalk
13	% identification but does \emph{not} ship the bridge itself. The bridge is
14	% \emph{not} a published-textbook lemma; it is the project-side assembly of
15	% four standard Mathlib API pieces:
16	%   1. \texttt{Order.coheight\_orderIso} -- coheight transport across order
17	%      isomorphisms (Mathlib \texttt{Order.KrullDimension}).
18	%   2. \texttt{Topology.Inseparable.Specializes.mem\_open} -- opens contain
19	%      every generisation of their points (Mathlib \texttt{Topology.Inseparable}).
20	%   3. \texttt{IsLocalization.AtPrime.ringKrullDim\_eq\_height} -- the algebra
21	%      bridge between localisation Krull dim and prime-ideal height
22	%      (Mathlib \texttt{RingTheory.Ideal.Height}).
23	%   4. \texttt{IsAffineOpen.isLocalization\_stalk} -- the affine-stalk
24	%      \(=\) localisation identification (Mathlib \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme}).
25	% The Lean target file \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean}
26	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
27	% creating it. None of the four blocks below has a direct Hartshorne / Milne /
28	% Mumford / Stacks tag that quotes the exact statement, so the blocks are
29	% packaged as Archon-original assembly lemmas with explicit Mathlib API
30	% pointers in lieu of \texttt{\% SOURCE QUOTE:} blocks.
31	
32	\section{Setup and motivation}
33	\label{sec:coheight_setup}
34	
35	A point \(z\) of a scheme \(X\) has two natural ``dimension'' invariants attached
36	to it. The \emph{Krull dimension of the local ring at \(z\)}, written
37	\(\dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z}\), is an algebraic quantity: the supremum of the lengths
38	of chains of prime ideals in the stalk \(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}\). The
39	\emph{coheight} of \(z\) in \(X\), written \(\operatorname{coheight}_X(z)\), is a
40	topological quantity attached to the specialisation preorder on the underlying
41	topological space of \(X\): the supremum of the lengths of strict-generisation
42	chains \(z = a_0 \prec a_1 \prec \cdots \prec a_n\) in \(X\), where \(\prec\) is the
43	strict specialisation order \(x \prec y \iff y \rightsquigarrow x \wedge y \neq x\).
44	
45	These two invariants agree:
46	\[
47	  \dim \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;=\; \operatorname{coheight}_X(z),
48	\]
49	which is the headline theorem \cref{thm:ringKrullDim_stalk_eq_coheight} of this
50	chapter. The identification is folklore in algebraic geometry --- it is the
51	statement that the dimension of the local ring at \(z\) counts irreducible
52	closed subsets of \(X\) containing \(z\) --- but at the project's pinned Mathlib
53	commit (\texttt{b80f227}) the equation is not packaged as a single declaration
54	and must be assembled project-side from four Mathlib API pieces.
55	
56	\paragraph{Why the bridge is needed.} Two downstream files in the project
57	currently work around the missing bridge by threading explicit
58	\texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk \_)]} instance arguments at every
59	codim-\(1\) use site:
60	\begin{itemize}
61	  \item \cref{chap:Albanese_CodimOneExtension} carries an internal conjunction
62	    \[
63	      \text{hreg\_dim : IsRegularLocalRing\ } \mathcal{O}_{X,z} \;\wedge\;
64	      \operatorname{ringKrullDim}(\mathcal{O}_{X,z}) = 1
65	    \]
66	    inside the private helper
67	    \texttt{smooth\_codim\_one\_maximalIdeal\_isPrincipal\_and\_ne\_bot};
68	    the Krull-dim conjunct is supplied by \emph{this chapter's}
69	    \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one}, leaving only the
70	    irreducible \texttt{IsRegularLocalRing} half (which is the separate
71	    Stacks tag \texttt{00TT} sub-project, out of scope here).
72	  \item \cref{chap:RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor}'s order-at-a-prime-divisor map
73	    \texttt{Scheme.RationalMap.order} currently carries an explicit
74	    \texttt{[Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]} instance argument;
75	    after \cref{lem:ringKrullDimLE_of_coheight_eq_one} lands, the instance is
76	    synthesisable from the codim-\(1\) hypothesis on \(Y\) alone, so the explicit
77	    argument can be dropped.
78	\end{itemize}
79

1	\chapter{Milne Theorem 3.2: Rational maps into abelian varieties}
2	\label{chap:Albanese_Thm32RationalMapExtension}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/Thm32RationalMapExtension.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.4.c sub-row of Route A
6	% (the positive-genus arm of \texttt{nonempty\_jacobianWitness}). It is the

12	% chapter is Milne's Theorem~3.2 of Chapter~I~\S 3 of \emph{Abelian Varieties}:
13	% \emph{every rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety
14	% extends, uniquely, to a regular morphism}. This is the input that A.4.d
15	% (\cref{chap:Albanese_AlbaneseUP}) consumes to upgrade the symmetric-product
16	% rational map \(C^{(g)} \dashrightarrow A\) to a regular morphism \(J \to A\),
17	% which in turn yields the Albanese universal property of \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\)
18	% (Milne III~\S 6, Proposition~6.1).
19	%
20	% This chapter is also the canonical home of the Lean target
21	% \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend\_to\_av}. A placeholder
22	% lemma block \texttt{lem:rational\_map\_to\_av\_extends} was reserved
23	% earlier in \cref{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity} (under the alternative Lean

27	%

29	% does not yet exist; this chapter is the prover-ready specification for
30	% creating it.
31	
32	\section{Setup and statement}
33	\label{sec:thm32_setup}
34	
35	Fix an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), a smooth variety \(X\) over \(\bar k\) (in the
36	project's convention: a geometrically integral, geometrically reduced, separated scheme of
37	finite type over \(\bar k\) that is moreover smooth, hence nonsingular at every closed point),
38	and an abelian variety \(A\) over \(\bar k\) (in particular \(A\) is complete, smooth, and a
39	group object in \(\mathrm{Sch}/\bar k\)). A rational map
40	\(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) is, in the project's notation, the equivalence class of a
41	regular morphism \(\varphi_U \colon U \to A\) defined on a dense open subset
42	\(U \subseteq X\), two such pairs \((U, \varphi_U)\) and \((U', \varphi_{U'})\) being equivalent
43	if \(\varphi_U\) and \(\varphi_{U'}\) agree on \(U \cap U'\).
44	
45	The result of this chapter is:
46	
47	\begin{theorem}
48	
49	\leanok
50	[Milne Theorem~3.2: a rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends]
51	  \label{thm:rational_map_to_av_extends}
52	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.extend_to_av}
53	  \uses{thm:codim_one_extension, lem:milne_codim1_indeterminacy}
54	
55	  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, doc p.~17
56	  % (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 23).
57	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
58	  % "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map \(\alpha\colon V--\to A\) from a nonsingular
59	  %  variety to an abelian variety is defined on the whole of \(V\)."
60	  \textit{Source: Milne, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Theorem~3.2, \S I.3, p.~17.}
61	
62	  Let \(X\) be a nonsingular variety over an algebraically closed field \(\bar k\), let \(A\) be
63	  an abelian variety over \(\bar k\), and let \(f \colon X \dashrightarrow A\) be a rational
64	  map. Then \(f\) is defined on the whole of \(X\): there is a unique regular morphism
65	  \(\widetilde f \colon X \to A\) whose restriction to some (equivalently, every) dense open
66	  set of definition \(U \subseteq X\) of \(f\) agrees with \(\varphi_U\).
67	\end{theorem}
68	
69	The uniqueness clause is immediate from \(X\) being reduced and separated: two regular
70	morphisms \(X \to A\) that agree on a dense open subset agree everywhere. The substance is
71	existence of the extension. Milne deduces it in one line by combining the codim-\(\geq 2\)
72	extension result for rational maps to a complete variety with the codim-1 indeterminacy
73	structure result for rational maps to a group variety:
74	
75	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
76	% "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma."
77	\begin{proof}
78	  
79	  Milne's one-line proof is: \emph{combine Theorem~3.1 with Lemma~3.3}.

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
5	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
6	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
7	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
8	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
9	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application},
10	% \texttt{lemma-flat-base-change-cohomology}) (read from
11	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
12	
13	\section{Setup and motivation}
14	
15	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
16	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
17	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
18	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
19	derived functor, which in a formalisation requires the ambient category of
20	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives; that property is not currently
21	available for sheaves of modules over a sheaf of rings whose value category varies
22	over the site. The {\v C}ech approach developed here sidesteps the issue entirely:
23	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
24	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
25	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
26	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
27	
28	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
29	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
30	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
31	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
32	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
33	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
34	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
35	\[
36	  \begin{array}{ccc}
37	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
38	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
39	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
40	  \end{array}
41	\]
42	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
43	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
44	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
45	
46	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
47	
48	\begin{definition}
49	
50	\leanok
51	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
52	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
53	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
54	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, lem:push_pull_functor}
55	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
56	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
57	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
58	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
59	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
60	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
61	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
62	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
63	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
64	  % the unit ideal, see
65	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
66	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
67	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
68	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
69	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
70	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
71	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
72	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
73	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
74	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
75	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
76	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
77	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34

52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the

9	\section{Setup and motivation}
10	
11	This chapter treats the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
12	\(i \geq 1\), complementing the \(i = 0\) (direct-image) case of
13	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}. These higher direct images are the
14	single deepest foundation of the cohomology-and-base-change package: the
15	Cohen--Macaulay regularity bound, semicontinuity of fibre cohomology, and the
16	\(m\)-regularity estimate of FGA all rest on the basic finiteness and base-change
17	properties of \(R^i f_*\) recorded here.
18	
19	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category) and a
20	{\v C}ech-complex approach, parallel to the \(i = 0\) treatment of
21	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}.
22	
23	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
24	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
25	A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded by the
26	cartesian square obtained from the fibre product \(X' = X \times_S S'\):
27	\[
28	  \begin{array}{ccc}
29	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
30	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
31	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
32	  \end{array}
33	\]
34	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
35	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
36	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, \(k\) denotes a field and \(C\) a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over \(k\) of relative dimension \(1\). In other words, \(C \to \Spec k\) is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition \(g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\); see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
18	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
19	  
20	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve \(C\) over \(k\) is the natural number
21	  \[
22	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
23	  \]
24	  the \(k\)-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of \(C\).
25	\end{definition}
26	
27	\begin{proof}
28	  
29	  \leanok
30	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules construction and applying the \(k\)-linear sheaf cohomology \(H^1\). The result is a \(k\)-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
31	
32	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
33	    The formal definition uses the project's \(\ModuleCat k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
34	    \[
35	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
36	    \]
37	    The carrier \(\toModuleKSheaf C\) is the sheaf-of-\(k\)-modules constructed from \(\struct C\) via the structure morphism; the wrapper \(\HModule k F 1\) is the \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\), which carries a canonical \(\Module k\)-structure inherited from the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category.
38	  \end{remark}
39	
40	  Mathlib's \(\Module.\mathtt{finrank}\) returns the \(k\)-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper \(k\)-scheme guarantees \(\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty\), so \(g(C)\) is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
41	\end{proof}
42	
43	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
44	\begin{itemize}
45	  \item \(g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})\) (Serre duality).
46	  \item \(g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)\), where \(\chi\) is the Euler characteristic.
47	  \item For a smooth projective curve over \(\mathbb C\), \(g(C)\) equals the topological genus of \(C(\mathbb C)\).
48	\end{itemize}
49	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
50	
51	\section{Mathlib gap}
52	\label{sec:Genus_status}
53	
54	The honest definition \(\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)\) requires a \(\Module k\)-flavoured sheaf cohomology of \(\struct C\) on the topology of opens of the underlying space of \(C\). Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\AddCommGrpCat\)-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as \(\Ext\) from the constant sheaf at \(\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)\).
58	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel \(\Module k\)-valued construction with unit object \(\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k\) instead of \(\ULift\,\mathbb Z\), gluing through the \(\Linear k\)-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a \(\Module k\) structure on the cohomology groups.
59	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a \(\ModuleCat k\)-valued sheaf} (this project): for \(C\) over \(\Spec k\), the sheafification of the presheaf \(U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))\) where the \(k\)-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
60	  \item \textbf{\(H^0\) algebraic bridge} (this project): \(H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)\), via Mathlib's \(\Ext\) linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content \(H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k\) on a connected proper \(k\)-curve.

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.

59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert
66	functor \(\Hilb_{C/k}(T)\), which is the special case \(\Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\)
67	of the Quot functor of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}; and it determines an
68	invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}(D)\) on the relative curve,
69	representing a class in \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\).
